advantages: Models the widest range of relationships — entities, dependencies, networks, decisions.
Adjacency lists are O(V + E), so sparse graphs scale to millions of edges in memory.
Same algorithm skeleton (worklist + visited set) handles many problems by swapping the ADT.
Foundation for vector-search indexes, agent orchestration, dependency resolvers and many ML systems.
disadvantages: More moving parts than trees: cycles, directionality and weights all need explicit handling.
Adjacency matrix is O(V²) space — wasteful for sparse graphs.
Deep graphs blow Python's recursion limit; you often need iterative traversals.
Algorithms have correctness preconditions (Dijkstra needs non-negative weights; topo-sort needs no cycles).
